User.title: User
User.description: Represents a user profile in the SyncDoc application. This entity stores basic user information, assuming an external system handles authentication credentials like passwords.

id.description: Unique identifier for the User entity.

email.description: The user's email address, used for login and notifications. This must be unique across all users.

name.description: The user's display name or full name.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the user account was created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the user account was last updated.

Document.title: Document
Document.description: Represents a collaborative document in the SyncDoc application, allowing multiple users to edit its content in real-time.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Document entity.

title.description: The title of the document, displayed on the dashboard and workspace.

content.description: The rich text content of the document, typically stored as a string (e.g., JSON or TipTap-specific format).

ownerId.description: Reference to the User who originally created and owns this document. (Relationship: User 1:N Document)

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the document was created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the document was last updated.

Permission.title: Permission
Permission.description: Defines the specific access level (role) a user has on a particular document, facilitating Role-Based Access Control.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Permission entry.

userId.description: Reference to the User who is granted this specific permission. (Relationship: User N:N Document via Permission)

documentId.description: Reference to the Document for which this permission is granted. (Relationship: User N:N Document via Permission)

role.description: The role assigned to the user for this document, e.g., 'Owner', 'Editor', or 'Viewer'.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when this permission was granted.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp indicating when this permission was last updated.

Message.title: Message
Message.description: Represents a chat message sent within the real-time chat sidebar of a document.

id.description: Unique identifier for the chat message.

documentId.description: Reference to the Document where this message was sent. (Relationship: Document 1:N Message)

userId.description: Reference to the User who sent this message. (Relationship: User 1:N Message)

content.description: The textual content of the chat message.

fileId.description: Optional reference to a File uploaded and associated with this message. (Relationship: File 1:1 Message)

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the message was sent.

File.title: File
File.description: Represents an image or PDF file uploaded by a user within a document's context, typically appearing in the chat feed.

id.description: Unique identifier for the uploaded file.

documentId.description: Reference to the Document this file is associated with. (Relationship: Document 1:N File)

userId.description: Reference to the User who uploaded this file. (Relationship: User 1:N File)

url.description: The public URL where the file is stored (e.g., Cloudinary URL).

filename.description: The original name of the uploaded file.

fileType.description: The type of the file, such as 'image' or 'pdf'.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the file was uploaded.

definition.description: Stores user profile information. Access is typically restricted to the user themselves (read/write their own profile) or backend services. The `{userId}` wildcard directly corresponds to `request.auth.uid` for security rules.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique ID of the user, corresponding to `request.auth.uid`.

definition.description: Stores collaborative document content and metadata. This collection includes denormalized authorization fields: `ownerId` (the user who created it) and a `members` map (e.g., `{'uid1': 'owner', 'uid2': 'editor', 'uid3': 'viewer'}`) for efficient, atomic role-based access control. This design enables secure 'list' operations for all documents a user has access to, without requiring additional `get()` calls in security rules. The `Permission` entity's data is reflected in this `members` map.

params.name: documentId
params.description: The unique ID of the document.

definition.description: Stores chat messages associated with a specific document. Each message document denormalizes `documentOwnerId` and `documentMembers` from its parent document to ensure Authorization Independence. This allows security rules to evaluate access to messages without performing a `get()` call on the parent document, thereby supporting secure 'list' operations.

params.name: documentId
params.description: The unique ID of the parent document.

params.name: messageId
params.description: The unique ID of the message.

definition.description: Stores metadata for files uploaded to a specific document. Each file document denormalizes `documentOwnerId` and `documentMembers` from its parent document to ensure Authorization Independence. This allows security rules to evaluate access to files without performing a `get()` call on the parent document, thereby supporting secure 'list' operations.

params.name: documentId
params.description: The unique ID of the parent document.

params.name: fileId
params.description: The unique ID of the file.

firestore.reasoning:
This Firestore structure is designed to strictly adhere to the core principles of Authorization Independence and enable secure QAPs (Queryable Authorization Patterns), making security rules simple, robust, and debuggable. 

**Authorization Independence via Denormalization (CRITICAL):**
1.  **Documents Collection (`/documents/{documentId}`):** Each `Document` document includes a denormalized `members` map (e.g., `members: { 'uid1': 'owner', 'uid2': 'editor', 'uid3': 'viewer' }`). This map stores the roles of all users who have access to this specific document. This eliminates the need for `get()` calls to a separate `permissions` collection within security rules. When a user creates or is granted access to a document, the backend will ensure this `members` map is updated.
2.  **Subcollections (`/documents/{documentId}/messages/{messageId}` and `/documents/{documentId}/files/{fileId}`):** Documents within these subcollections (`Message`, `File`) also denormalize the `documentOwnerId` and `documentMembers` map from their parent `Document`. This means that security rules for messages and files do not need to perform a `get()` operation on the parent document. All necessary authorization context is present directly within the `message` or `file` document itself. This is critical for maintaining atomic operations and preventing security rule complexity.

**QAPs (Rules are not Filters):**
1.  **Listing User-Accessible Documents:** Users can efficiently query for all documents they have access to by performing a `list` operation on the `/documents` collection. A Firestore query like `db.collection('documents').where('members.{{request.auth.uid}}', '!=', null)` will return all documents where the authenticated user is listed in the `members` map, without requiring security rules to filter data. The security rules will only need to verify that `request.auth.uid` exists in `resource.data.members`.
2.  **Listing Messages/Files within a Document:** For subcollections like `messages` or `files`, listing is secured by the denormalized `documentMembers` map within each message or file document. If a user has read access to the parent document (checked via `documentMembers` on the subcollection document), they can list all contained messages or files. This allows for secure `list` operations without relying on slow or complex `get()` calls in rules.

**DBAC (Database-Based Access Control):** Roles are explicitly stored within the `members` map on `Document`, `Message`, and `File` documents, ensuring that all authorization decisions are made based on data stored directly in the database, aligned with `request.auth.uid`.

This design prioritizes high performance and maintainability by embedding authorization logic directly into the data structure, significantly reducing the complexity and potential for errors in Firestore Security Rules.
